{"step_by_step_thinking": "Subdural empyema can result from the direct extension of local infections, such as those originating in the paranasal sinuses. Multiple documents confirm that subdural empyema often arises from infections in the head and neck region, particularly from sinusitis. Document [5] explicitly states that intracranial complications of acute sinusitis include subdural empyema, especially when the infection spreads through the valveless diploic veins. Document [13] notes that up to 40% to 80% of patients with subdural empyema have otorhinologic infections, especially of the paranasal sinuses. Document [18] states that subdural empyema may start from infections spreading from nearby structures such as the paranasal sinuses, mastoid air cells, or the middle ear. These findings consistently support that sinusitis can be a precursor or source of subdural empyema. Therefore, based on the evidence, subdural empyema is indeed a complication of sinusitis.", "answer_choice": "A"}
